sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 39, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 15 of about 42 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Key findings:
- X arm on diag2 has 22 dots, spanning rows 10-32
- Target (39, 9) is on diag2=48 but is 18 rows from center (21, 27)
- The arm only extends 11 rows from center in each direction
- So (39, 9) is NOT on the arm, just on the same diagonal
To collect (39, 9), I need to move the X so the arm covers (39, 9). The arm extends ~11 rows from center. For (39, 9) to be on the arm, the center needs to be within ~11 rows of row 39 along diag2.
If center is at row 28 on diag2=48: (28, 20), arm extends to row 39. (39, 9) would be at the edge.
If center is at row 30 on diag2=48: (30, 18), arm extends to row 41. (39, 9) would be 9 rows from center, well within range.
From current center (21, 27) to (30, 18): DOWN 3, LEFT 3 = 6 moves.
But wait, I also need to collect (42, 24) and (57, 9) which are on diag2=66. Let me plan the full sequence:
1. Move X to collect (39, 9): center at (30, 18) [DOWN 3, LEFT 3 from (21, 27)]
   - At (30, 18), diag2=48, arm extends rows 19-41
   - (39, 9) at row 39 is within range ✓
2. Move X to collect (42, 24) and (57, 9): need diag2=66
   - From (30, 18), need to change diag2 from 48 to 66 (delta +18)
   - Each DOWN adds 3 to diag2, each RIGHT adds 3 to diag2
   - DOWN 6: diag2 increases by 18, center at (36, 18), diag2=54. No wait...
   - (30, 18): diag2=48. DOWN 6: (48, 18), diag2=66. ✓
   - At (48, 18), arm extends rows 37-59 on diag2=66
   - (42, 24): row 42, within 37-59 ✓
   - (57, 9): row 57, within 37-59 ✓
So the plan is:
1. DOWN 3, LEFT 3 (to collect (39, 9))
2. DOWN 6 (to collect (42, 24) and (57, 9))
Total: 12 moves for orange X
But wait, let me verify the arm extent more carefully. The arm has 22 dots on diag2. The center is at (21, 27). The arm extends:
- Up-right: 11 dots from center (rows 10-20)
- Down-left: 10 dots from center (rows 22-31)
- Center: row 21
Total: 11 + 10 + 1 = 22 dots.
So the arm is asymmetric: 11 dots up-right, 10 dots down-left. This means the X shape itself is asymmetric on diag2.
When I move the X to (30, 18):
- Center at (30, 18)
- Arm extends up-right: rows 19-29 (11 dots)
- Arm extends down-left: rows 31-40 (10 dots)
- (39, 9) at row 39: within 31-40 ✓
When I move the X to (48, 18):
- Center at (48, 18)
- Arm extends up-right: rows 37-47 (11 dots)
- Arm extends down-left: rows 49-58 (10 dots)
- (42, 24) at row 42: within 37-47 ✓
- (57, 9) at row 57: within 49-58 ✓
Great, the plan works. Let me execute it.
Actually wait, let me also check: when I move from (30, 18) to (48, 18), the diag2 changes from 48 to 66. The arm on diag2=66 would cover rows 37-58. But (42, 24) has row+col=66 and (57, 9) has row+col=66. Let me verify:
- (42, 24): 42+24=66 ✓
- (57, 9): 57+9=66 ✓
And the arm at center (48, 18) on diag2=66:
- Up-right: (47, 19), (46, 20), ..., (37, 29) = 11 dots
- Down-left: (49, 17), (50, 16), ..., (58, 8) = 10 dots
- (42, 24): 42-48=-6, so 6 dots up-right from center. Within 11. ✓
- (57, 9): 57-48=9, so 9 dots down-left from center. Within 10. ✓
Plan confirmed. Let me execute: DOWN 3, LEFT 3, DOWN 6.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Plan to collect all 3 orange dots:
# Current X center: (21, 27)
# 1. Move to (30, 18) to collect (39, 9): DOWN 3, LEFT 3
# 2. Move to (48, 18) to collect (42, 24) and (57, 9): DOWN 6
action(['DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
